sections.title: Criteria for determining significant user numbers or market threats
sections.summary: The Commission must adopt delegated acts to specify criteria for determining significant numbers of payment service users or threats to market stability. These criteria include complexity, riskiness, fraud potential, uptake levels, and innovation levels of payment services. This applies to the Commission when creating delegated acts.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines criteria for assessing significant user numbers and market threats, critical for cross-border payment service risk assessments.

requirements.text: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union

The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument

sections.source_quote: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union referred to in paragraph 2, point (a). Those criteria and factors shall include: (a) the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered; (b) the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (c) the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (d) the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (e) the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument.

sections.title: Payment initiation service provider interface requirements
sections.summary: Payment initiation service providers must identify themselves, use the account servicing provider's authentication, restrict data processing to the requested service, and log accessed data for 3 years with deletion after 3 years unless needed for ongoing monitoring. These obligations apply to payment initiation service providers interacting with account servicing payment service providers via interfaces.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires payment initiation service providers to maintain strict data handling protocols when accessing payment account data through interfaces, including logging and timely deletion of access logs to ensure data minimization and accountability in cross-border payment processing.

requirements.text: Identify itself towards the account servicing payment service provider
Rely on the authentication procedures provided by the account servicing payment service provider to the payment service user
Take necessary measures to ensure data processing is only for the service requested by the payment service user
Log data accessed through the interface for payment service users
Provide log files to the competent authority upon request without undue delay
Delete logs 3 years after creation
Keep logs longer than 3 years if required for ongoing monitoring procedures

sections.source_quote: Where an account information service provider or a payment initiation service provider accesses payment account data via an interface that the account servicing payment service provider makes available to its payment service users for directly accessing their payment account, in accordance with Article 38(4) and (5), or where that is the only interface accessible in accordance with Article 39, the account information service provider or the payment initiation service provider shall at all times: (a) identify itself towards the account servicing payment service provider; (b) rely on the authentication procedures provided by the account servicing payment service provider to the payment service user; (c) take the necessary measures to ensure that they do not process data (including access and storage of data) for purposes other than for the provision of the service as requested by the payment service user; (d) log the data that are accessed through the interface operated by the account servicing payment service provider for its payment service users, and provide, upon request and without undue delay, the log files to the competent authority. Logs shall be deleted 3 years after their creation. Logs may be kept for longer than this retention period if they are required for monitoring procedures that are already underway.

sections.title: EBA action safeguards for payment services
sections.summary: When taking action under this article, the EBA must ensure the action does not have disproportionate effects on market efficiency, does not create regulatory arbitrage risks, and has been consulted with relevant national competent authorities. This applies to the EBA when implementing precautionary measures on payment services.
sections.dtia_summary: Safeguards for EBA actions ensure actions on payment services do not disrupt markets or create regulatory arbitrage risks.

requirements.text: The action does not have a detrimental effect on the efficiency of the payments market or electronic money services market or on payment services or electronic money service providers that is disproportionate to the benefits of the action
The action does not create a risk of regulatory arbitrage
The action has been taken after consulting the relevant national competent authority

sections.source_quote: When taking action under this Article, the EBA shall ensure all of the following: (a) the action does not have a detrimental effect on the efficiency of the payments market or electronic money services market or on payment services or electronic money service providers that is disproportionate to the benefits of the action; (b) the action does not create a risk of regulatory arbitrage, and (c) the action has been taken after consulting the relevant national competent authority.

sections.heading: Competent authorities shall cooperate with supervisory authorities under Regulation
sections.title: Remote mandate setup: strong customer authentication requirement
sections.summary: When a payer provides a mandate to a payee via a remote channel involving a payment service provider, strong customer authentication is required for setting up the mandate. This applies to payment orders for transactions specified in paragraph 3.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires SCA for remote mandate setup involving payment service providers, impacting cross-border payment processing security.

requirements.text: Setting up of such a mandate shall be subject to strong customer authentication

sections.source_quote: Where the mandate of the payer to the payee to place payment orders for transactions referred to in paragraph 3 is provided through a remote channel with the involvement of the payment service provider, the setting up of such a mandate shall be subject to strong customer authentication.

sections.heading: Competent authorities shall, on their initiative, hold regular joint meetings with
sections.title: Direct debits with remote setup: strong customer authentication requirement
sections.summary: For direct debits where the payer's mandate is provided through a remote channel with payment service provider involvement, strong customer authentication must be applied. This applies to mandates for one or several direct debit transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: Mandates for direct debits require SCA when set up remotely with payment service provider involvement, affecting cross-border data transfer security.

requirements.text: Strong customer authentication shall be applied

sections.source_quote: For direct debits, where the mandate given by the payer to the payee to initiate one or several direct debit transactions is provided through a remote channel with the direct involvement of a payment service provider in the setting up of such a mandate, strong customer authentication shall be applied.

sections.heading: Account servicing payment service providers shall provide competent authorities
sections.title: Non-electronic payment orders: exemption from strong customer authentication
sections.summary: Payment transactions using non-electronic modalities like paper-based orders or telephone orders are exempt from strong customer authentication. This exemption applies regardless of electronic execution, provided payment service providers conduct security checks and authentication.
sections.dtia_summary: Exempts non-electronic payment orders from SCA requirements, reducing compliance burden for traditional payment methods.

requirements.text: Payment transactions placed by the payer with modalities other than electronic platforms shall not be subject to strong customer authentication

sections.source_quote: Payment transactions for which payment orders are placed by the payer with modalities other than the use of electronic platforms or devices, such as paper-based payment orders, mail orders or telephone orders, shall not be subject to strong customer authentication, irrespective of whether or not the execution of the transaction is performed electronically, provided that security requirements and checks are carried out by the payment service provider of the payer allowing a form of authentication of the payment transaction.

sections.heading: The EBA shall develop draft regulatory technical standards specifying the data to be
sections.title: Criteria for determining significant user numbers or market threats
sections.summary: The Commission must adopt delegated acts to specify criteria for determining significant numbers of payment service users or threats to market stability. These criteria include complexity, riskiness, fraud potential, uptake levels, and innovation levels of payment services. This applies to the Commission when creating delegated acts.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines criteria for assessing significant user numbers and market threats, critical for cross-border payment service risk assessments.

requirements.text: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union

The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument

sections.source_quote: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union referred to in paragraph 2, point (a). Those criteria and factors shall include: (a) the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered; (b) the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (c) the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (d) the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (e) the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument.

sections.title: Retention of published data on official websites
sections.summary: Competent authorities must ensure that any publications made under GDPR enforcement procedures remain on their official website for up to 5 years. Personal data in these publications must be retained only if an annual review confirms continued necessity for financial market stability or regulatory enforcement, and for no longer than 5 years.

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities shall ensure that any publication made in accordance with this Article remains on their official website for a period of up to 5 years. Personal data contained in the publication shall be kept on the official website of the competent authority only if an annual review shows the continued need to publish that data to protect the stability of the financial markets or to ensure the effective enforcement of this Regulation, and in any event for no longer than 5 years.

These criteria include complexity, riskiness, fraud potential, uptake levels, and innovation levels of payment services. This applies to the Commission when creating delegated acts.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines criteria for assessing significant user numbers and market threats, critical for cross-border payment service risk assessments.

requirements.text: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union

The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument

sections.source_quote: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union referred to in paragraph 2, point (a). Those criteria and factors shall include: (a) the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered; (b) the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (c) the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (d) the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (e) the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument.

sections.title: Retention of published data on official websites
sections.summary: Competent authorities must ensure that any publications made under GDPR enforcement procedures remain on their official website for up to 5 years. Personal data in these publications must be retained only if an annual review confirms continued necessity for financial market stability or regulatory enforcement, and for no longer than 5 years.

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities shall ensure that any publication made in accordance with this Article remains on their official website for a period of up to 5 years. Personal data contained in the publication shall be kept on the official website of the competent authority only if an annual review shows the continued need to publish that data to protect the stability of the financial markets or to ensure the effective enforcement of this Regulation, and in any event for no longer than 5 years.

sections.heading: Payment transactions for which payment orders are placed by the payer with
sections.title: Non-electronic payment orders: exemption from strong customer authentication
sections.summary: Payment transactions using non-electronic modalities like paper-based orders or telephone orders are exempt from strong customer authentication. This exemption applies regardless of electronic execution, provided payment service providers conduct security checks and authentication.
sections.dtia_summary: Exempts non-electronic payment orders from SCA requirements, reducing compliance burden for traditional payment methods.

requirements.text: Payment transactions placed by the payer with modalities other than electronic platforms shall not be subject to strong customer authentication

sections.source_quote: Payment transactions for which payment orders are placed by the payer with modalities other than the use of electronic platforms or devices, such as paper-based payment orders, mail orders or telephone orders, shall not be subject to strong customer authentication, irrespective of whether or not the execution of the transaction is performed electronically, provided that security requirements and checks are carried out by the payment service provider of the payer allowing a form of authentication of the payment transaction.

sections.heading: For the remote placement of a payment order as referred to in paragraph 1, point (c),
sections.title: Criteria for determining significant user numbers or market threats
sections.summary: The Commission must adopt delegated acts to specify criteria for determining significant numbers of payment service users or threats to market stability. These criteria include complexity, riskiness, fraud potential, uptake levels, and innovation levels of payment services. This applies to the Commission when creating delegated acts.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines criteria for assessing significant user numbers and market threats, critical for cross-border payment service risk assessments.

requirements.text: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union

The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument

sections.source_quote: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union referred to in paragraph 2, point (a). Those criteria and factors shall include: (a) the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered; (b) the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (c) the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (d) the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (e) the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument.

sections.heading: For the placement of a payment order as referred to in paragraph 1, point (c), through
sections.title: Proximity technology payment orders: strong customer authentication requirements
sections.summary: Payment service providers must apply strong customer authentication for payment orders placed via proximity technology with internet use on the payer's device. This authentication must dynamically link the transaction to a specific amount and payee or ensure equivalent security measures.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires SCA for proximity-based payment orders with internet use, ensuring transaction security across cross-border transfers.

requirements.text: Payment service providers shall apply strong customer authentication that includes elements which dynamically link the transaction to a specific amount and a specific payee or harmonised security measures of identical effect

sections.source_quote: For the placement of a payment order as referred to in paragraph 1, point (c), through a payer's device using proximity technology for the exchange of information with the payee's infrastructure, the authentication of which requires the use of internet on the payer's device, payment service providers shall apply strong customer authentication that includes elements which dynamically link the transaction to a specific amount and a specific payee or harmonised security measures of identical effect, which ensure the confidentiality, authenticity and integrity of the amount of the transaction and the payee throughout all of the phases of initiation.

sections.heading: For the purposes of paragraph 1, payment service providers shall have in place
sections.title: Payment service providers must implement security for user credentials
sections.summary: Payment service providers must establish adequate security measures to protect the confidentiality and integrity of users' personalised security credentials. These measures are required to safeguard payment service users' sensitive information. The obligation applies specifically to payment service providers handling user credentials.
sections.dtia_summary: Payment service providers must implement security measures for user credentials during cross-border data transfers involving payment services.

requirements.text: Payment service providers shall have in place adequate security measures to protect the confidentiality and integrity of payment service users' personalised security credentials

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of paragraph 1, payment service providers shall have in place adequate security measures to protect the confidentiality and integrity of payment service users' personalised security credentials.

sections.heading: Any exemptions from the application of strong customer authentication to be
sections.title: Exemptions for strong customer authentication criteria
sections.summary: Exemptions from strong customer authentication requirements must be designed by the EBA under Article 89. These exemptions are based on risk level, transaction amount or recurrence, or payment channel used. The criteria must be explicitly defined by the EBA to determine applicable exemptions.
sections.dtia_summary: Exemptions from strong customer authentication require EBA-defined criteria for cross-border payment services, impacting data transfer security assessments.

requirements.text: Exemptions from the application of strong customer authentication to be designed by the EBA under Article 89 shall be based on one or more of the following criteria: the level of risk involved in the service provided

Exemptions from the application of strong customer authentication to be designed by the EBA under Article 89 shall be based on one or more of the following criteria: the amount, the recurrence of the transaction, or both

Exemptions from the application of strong customer authentication to be designed by the EBA under Article 89 shall be based on one or more of the following criteria: the payment channel used for the execution of the transaction

sections.source_quote: Any exemptions from the application of strong customer authentication to be designed by the EBA under Article 89 shall be based on one or more of the following criteria: (a) the level of risk involved in the service provided; (b) the amount, the recurrence of the transaction, or both; (c) the payment channel used for the execution of the transaction.

sections.heading: The two or more elements referred to in Article 3, point (35), on which strong
sections.title: Strong customer authentication elements independence
sections.summary: The elements used for strong customer authentication do not need to belong to different categories, but their independence must be fully preserved. This ensures that authentication mechanisms remain robust and effective without requiring diverse data types.
sections.dtia_summary: Strong customer authentication elements must maintain independence, which affects how data is securely transferred across borders in payment services.

requirements.text: The two or more elements referred to in Article 3, point (35), on which strong customer authentication shall be based do not necessarily need to belong to different categories

sections.source_quote: The two or more elements referred to in Article 3, point (35), on which strong customer authentication shall be based do not necessarily need to belong to different categories, as long as their independence is fully preserved.

sections.title: Retention of published data on official websites
sections.summary: Competent authorities must ensure that any publications made under GDPR enforcement procedures remain on their official website for up to 5 years. Personal data in these publications must be retained only if an annual review confirms continued necessity for financial market stability or regulatory enforcement, and for no longer than 5 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Published enforcement data must be retained for up to 5 years with annual reviews to ensure continued relevance, impacting data storage and retention periods for regulatory publications.

requirements.text: Published data must remain on the official website for up to 5 years

Personal data in publications must not be retained for longer than 5 years

sections.source_quote: Competent authorities shall ensure that any publication made in accordance with this Article remains on their official website for a period of up to 5 years. Personal data contained in the publication shall be kept on the official website of the competent authority only if an annual review shows the continued need to publish that data to protect the stability of the financial markets or to ensure the effective enforcement of this Regulation, and in any event for no longer than 5 years.

sections.title: EBA action safeguards for payment services
sections.summary: When taking action under this article, the EBA must ensure the action does not have disproportionate effects on market efficiency, does not create regulatory arbitrage risks, and has been consulted with relevant national competent authorities. This applies to the EBA when implementing precautionary measures on payment services.

sections.heading: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify
sections.title: Criteria for determining significant user numbers or market threats
sections.summary: The Commission must adopt delegated acts to specify criteria for determining significant numbers of payment service users or threats to market stability. These criteria include complexity, riskiness, fraud potential, uptake levels, and innovation levels of payment services. This applies to the Commission when creating delegated acts.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines criteria for assessing significant user numbers and market threats, critical for cross-border payment service risk assessments.

requirements.text: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union

The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument
The criteria and factors shall include: the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument

sections.source_quote: The Commission shall adopt delegated acts in accordance with Article 106 to specify criteria and factors to be taken into account by the EBA in determining when there is a significant number of payment services users or electronic money services users or a threat to the orderly functioning of the payment or electronic money services markets, and the integrity of these markets or to the stability of the whole or part of these markets in the Union referred to in paragraph 2, point (a). Those criteria and factors shall include: (a) the degree of complexity of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument and the relation to the type of users, including consumers, to whom they are offered; (b) the degree of riskiness, for consumers, of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (c) the possible use by fraudsters of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (d) the size or the level of uptake of the payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument; (e) the degree of innovation of a payment service or instrument or electronic money service or instrument.
